refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safe operation). The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' remains in the FR because it defines the functional boundary—the system must deliver latency sufficient for the tele-operator to safely command the equipment—without prescribing the specific numerical threshold (150 ms p95), which belongs in acceptance criteria or design constraints, not the FR itself. The FR is now atomic: it states a single functional obligation in the problem domain (what must be communicated and by whom) without specifying technology, protocol, or implementation approach.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials with representative tele-operators performing safety-critical tasks (e.g., precision positioning, obstacle avoidance, emergency stop) under agreed network conditions. Measure end-to-end latency (sensor-to-command-output) and correlate with task success rate, error frequency, and safety incident count. Verify that measured latency supports safe operation per worksite safety plan and operational policies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator commands to the remote equipment with measurable end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback to the tele-operator with measurable end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall support tele-operator situational awareness (visual, auditory, or haptic feedback) sufficient to enable safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Establish a dedicated, low-latency communication pathway with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and telemetry reach within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget.

DP2: Implement a hierarchical control architecture where local autonomous safety logic operates independently of teleoperation link, with teleoperation commands layered as supervisory inputs that are validated against safety constraints before execution.

DP3: Decompose teleoperation into separate functional channels (command uplink, telemetry downlink, heartbeat/watchdog) with independent buffering and retry logic, allowing each to be optimized for latency without coupling.

DP4: Design a latency-aware operator interface that provides predictive feedback (dead-reckoning, local echo) to mask network delays, decoupling perceived responsiveness from actual round-trip latency.

DP5: Establish a network abstraction layer that isolates teleoperation logic from specific transport media, enabling transparent failover and multi-path routing without redesign of command/telemetry protocols.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP3 + DP5 (integrated approach): Implement a hierarchical control architecture where (1) local autonomous safety logic operates independently of the teleoperation link with its own watchdog and constraint enforcement, (2) teleoperation is decomposed into independent functional channels (command uplink, telemetry downlink, heartbeat) each with dedicated buffering and latency optimization, and (3) a network abstraction layer isolates teleoperation logic from transport specifics, enabling adaptive routing and failover without protocol redesign. This design ensures that latency violations trigger safe fallback (not system failure) and that communication pathways can be independently tuned.

potential_sources: Coupling between command validation logic and safety constraint enforcement: mitigated by clear interface contract (safety logic accepts/rejects commands, teleoperation layer does not bypass).
Coupling between telemetry downlink and operator interface: mitigated by DP4 (predictive feedback decouples perceived latency from actual latency).
Coupling between network abstraction and command/telemetry protocols: mitigated by DP5 (abstraction layer shields protocol from transport details).
Potential coupling between heartbeat/watchdog and command processing: acceptable if heartbeat is independent channel with its own timeout semantics.
coupling_check.notes: The integrated DP minimizes coupling by enforcing clear separation of concerns: safety logic is autonomous, communication channels are independent, and transport is abstracted. Risk of coupling arises only if safety constraints are embedded in teleoperation layer or if heartbeat logic interferes with command buffering; both are design-time decisions that can be enforced.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous safety logic can be designed to operate safely without real-time teleoperation input (i.e., safe fallback state is defined and achievable).
ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are characterized sufficiently to set QoS parameters; 'agreed network conditions' must be formally specified (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, topology).

ASSUMPTION: Operator training and worksite safety plan account for latency-induced delays in operator perception and response; 150 ms p95 is acceptable for the intended task (e.g., not suitable for high-speed precision tasks).
RISK: If local safety logic is incomplete or incorrectly specified, system may enter unsafe state despite teleoperation safeguards.
RISK: If network abstraction layer introduces additional latency (e.g., through extra buffering or routing logic), p95 budget may be exceeded; abstraction must be latency-transparent.
RISK: Heartbeat/watchdog timeout must be tuned carefully; too short causes false positives (unnecessary fallback), too long delays detection of link failure.
RISK: Operator interface predictive feedback (DP4) may create false sense of responsiveness, leading to unsafe operator behavior if actual latency exceeds prediction window.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'safe remote operation' in terms of fallback behavior when teleoperation link fails or latency exceeds threshold, (2) Characterization of 'agreed network conditions' (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, topology, failover strategy), (3) Operator task profile and latency sensitivity (e.g., is 150 ms acceptable for the intended manipulation tasks?), (4) Specification of local autonomous safety logic scope (what decisions can be made without teleoperation input?). Additionally, introduce a derived FR for watchdog/heartbeat timeout tuning and operator training requirements. This will enable DP evaluation against concrete acceptance criteria and reduce ambiguity in 'safe remote operation'.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates hierarchical control architecture integrating autonomous safety logic, decomposed teleoperation channels (command uplink, telemetry downlink, heartbeat), network abstraction layer, and adaptive routing/failover mechanisms. Owns all latency-critical buffering, constraint enforcement, watchdog logic, and transport-agnostic communication pathways.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands from remote station; routed through command uplink channel with latency optimization and constraint validation before Vehicle execution.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and system health to remote operator via dedicated downlink channel with independent buffering and adaptive routing.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety constraint interface: receives safety limits/watchdog parameters from Safety Services; broadcasts constraint violations and fallback state transitions to trigger autonomous safe behavior on Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstract transport interface enabling Teleoperation to operate independently of underlying protocol (RF, cellular, satellite, wired). Supports adaptive routing, failover, and latency monitoring without redesign of control logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. Teleoperation is the natural home for hierarchical control, channel decomposition, network abstraction, and latency-critical buffering. DP2 (autonomous safety logic) is co-located here as independent watchdog enforcement; DP3 (channel decomposition) is core to module purpose; DP5 (network abstraction) is essential for transport isolation. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services (external), and Vehicle receives fallback commands (expected cross-module dependency). Ownership is clear and cohesion is high.

Poor containment. Safety Services should own safety policy and constraint definitions, not teleoperation control logic. Placing hierarchical control, channel decomposition, and network abstraction here violates separation of concerns. DP2 (autonomous safety logic) has some affinity here, but DP3 and DP5 are fundamentally teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: command/telemetry buffering, latency optimization, and adaptive routing would leak into Vehicle and external transport layers. Not recommended.

Poor containment. Vehicle should own vehicle-side execution, actuation, and local state management, not remote teleoperation infrastructure. DP3 (channel decomposition) and DP5 (network abstraction) are pre-vehicle concerns. DP2 (autonomous safety logic) has some affinity to Vehicle (local watchdog), but the integrated hierarchical control architecture is a teleoperation-layer responsibility. High diffusion: command uplink, telemetry downlink, heartbeat channels, and network abstraction would be scattered across Vehicle and remote operator station. Not recommended.

Very poor containment. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing detailed hierarchical control, channel decomposition, and network abstraction here bypasses architectural layering and creates a monolithic, unmanageable boundary. All three DPs would diffuse into sub-modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services) without clear ownership. Not recommended.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Teleoperation module (owned by teleoperation/communication systems team or integrated vehicle control team with teleoperation specialization)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) is fundamentally a teleoperation-layer concern. Hierarchical control with autonomous safety fallback, channel decomposition, and network abstraction are all mechanisms to ensure reliable remote operation under latency and communication constraints. Teleoperation is the only module with sufficient scope and cohesion to contain all three DPs without major diffusion. Safety Services provides constraint policy (via SafetyAlert interface); Vehicle executes fallback commands; Network Link provides transport abstraction. This design maintains clear separation of concerns while achieving the required latency resilience and adaptive routing.
containment_risks: Risk: Autonomous safety logic (DP2) may be perceived as belonging to Safety Services rather than Teleoperation. Mitigation: Clarify that the watchdog and constraint enforcement in Teleoperation are *local* mechanisms to ensure safe fallback when teleoperation link fails; Safety Services owns global safety policy and constraint definitions. Both modules collaborate via SafetyAlert interface.

Risk: Network abstraction layer (DP5) may be confused with a separate networking module. Mitigation: Network abstraction is a logical layer within Teleoperation that decouples control logic from transport specifics. It is not a standalone module; it is an internal design pattern within Teleoperation that interfaces with Network Link (external transport).

Risk: Channel decomposition (DP3) may lead to code duplication if buffering/latency logic is replicated across command, telemetry, and heartbeat paths. Mitigation: Implement shared buffering and latency optimization utilities within Teleoperation module to avoid diffusion and ensure consistency.

Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns operator interface logic. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation owns only remote control protocol, channel management, and network abstraction; operator UI/UX is external to this module.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The integrated DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) is well-suited to Teleoperation module. However, clarify the internal architecture: (1) Autonomous safety logic should be documented as a *local watchdog* within Teleoperation, not a replacement for Safety Services; (2) Channel decomposition should be implemented via a shared buffering framework to avoid code duplication; (3) Network abstraction should be a logical layer (not a separate module) that adapts to Network Link interface. These clarifications improve containment and reduce diffusion risk.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns all latency-critical buffering, channel decomposition, and network abstraction mechanisms required to achieve this target. Verification can be conducted via end-to-end latency instrumentation across command uplink, telemetry downlink, and heartbeat channels under representative network conditions. The SafetyAlert interface provides observable fallback state transitions, enabling validation that safe behavior is triggered when latency violations occur.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to the Teleoperation IM boundary. The module owns hierarchical control architecture, autonomous safety watchdog, channel decomposition, and network abstraction—all mechanisms necessary to enable safe remote operation under latency constraints. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined, with clear directionality and responsibility boundaries. Safety Services provides constraint policy; Vehicle executes fallback; Network Link provides transport abstraction. No ambiguity in allocation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) directly addresses the FR requirement. DP2 (autonomous safety logic with independent watchdog) ensures safe fallback when teleoperation link fails, enabling safe remote operation despite latency violations. DP3 (decomposed functional channels with dedicated buffering) isolates command, telemetry, and heartbeat paths for independent latency optimization, reducing end-to-end latency. DP5 (network abstraction layer with adaptive routing/failover) decouples control logic from transport specifics, enabling dynamic path selection to minimize latency without protocol redesign. Together, these DPs form a coherent solution that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator under latency constraints. The design is logically sound and directly traceable to the FR.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Autonomous safety logic (DP2) may be perceived as a Safety Services responsibility rather than a Teleoperation-local mechanism. Mitigation: Clarify in design documentation that the watchdog and constraint enforcement in Teleoperation are *local* fallback mechanisms triggered by teleoperation link failure; Safety Services owns global safety policy and constraint definitions. Both modules collaborate via SafetyAlert interface with clear ownership boundaries.

Risk: Channel decomposition (DP3) may lead to code duplication if buffering and latency optimization logic is replicated across command uplink, telemetry downlink, and heartbeat paths. Mitigation: Implement a shared buffering and latency optimization framework within Teleoperation module (e.g., a common channel abstraction or utility library) to ensure consistency and avoid scattered implementation.

Risk: Network abstraction layer (DP5) may be confused with a separate networking module or conflated with the Network Link external interface. Mitigation: Document DP5 as a logical design pattern (adapter/facade) internal to Teleoperation that decouples control logic from transport specifics. Network Link is the external transport interface; network abstraction is the internal mechanism that adapts to it.

Risk: Teleoperation module scope may expand to include operator UI/UX logic, diluting focus on remote control protocol and channel management. Mitigation: Establish clear boundary: Teleoperation owns remote control protocol, channel management, latency optimization, and network abstraction; operator interface (UI, visualization, input handling) is external to this module.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The Teleoperation IM boundary is the natural and only appropriate home for the integrated DP. Hierarchical control architecture, channel decomposition, and network abstraction are fundamentally teleoperation-layer concerns. DP2 (autonomous safety logic) is co-located as a local watchdog mechanism independent of the teleoperation link, ensuring safe fallback without requiring Safety Services intervention. DP3 (channel decomposition) is core to the module's purpose of managing latency-critical communication pathways. DP5 (network abstraction) is essential for transport isolation and adaptive routing. Minor diffusion is expected and acceptable: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services (external policy provider), and Vehicle receives fallback commands (expected cross-module dependency). Ownership is clear, cohesion is high, and responsibilities do not leak into other modules. Alternative placements (Safety Services, Vehicle, NGHE Solution) result in poor containment (0.35, 0.28, 0.15 respectively) with high diffusion (0.65, 0.72, 0.85), violating separation of concerns and creating scattered implementation.

recommended_revisions: Clarify in design documentation that DP2 (autonomous safety logic) within Teleoperation is a *local watchdog* mechanism that enforces constraint fallback when the teleoperation link fails or latency exceeds thresholds. This is distinct from Safety Services, which owns global safety policy and constraint definitions. Both modules collaborate via the SafetyAlert interface: Safety Services provides constraint limits and watchdog parameters; Teleoperation enforces them locally and broadcasts violations to trigger Vehicle fallback.

Implement DP3 (channel decomposition) using a shared buffering and latency optimization framework within Teleoperation to avoid code duplication across command uplink, telemetry downlink, and heartbeat channels. Define a common channel abstraction (e.g., interface or base class) that encapsulates buffering, latency measurement, and adaptive scheduling logic. This ensures consistency and reduces diffusion risk.

Document DP5 (network abstraction layer) as a logical design pattern (adapter/facade) internal to Teleoperation, not as a separate module. Clarify that it decouples control logic from transport specifics by providing a unified interface to the Network Link external interface. This prevents confusion with networking modules and clarifies the boundary between internal design and external dependencies.

Establish and document a clear scope boundary for Teleoperation: owns remote control protocol, channel management, latency optimization, network abstraction, and autonomous safety watchdog; does not own operator UI/UX, vehicle actuation, or global safety policy. This prevents scope creep and maintains separation of concerns.

Add traceability from acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) to specific DP mechanisms: DP3 channel decomposition enables independent latency tuning per channel; DP5 network abstraction enables adaptive routing to select lowest-latency paths; DP2 autonomous watchdog ensures safe fallback if latency target is violated. Document how each DP contributes to meeting the latency requirement.

Define the SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly: (1) inputs from Safety Services (constraint limits, watchdog parameters, safety mode transitions); (2) outputs to Vehicle (fallback commands, constraint violation alerts, safe state transitions). This clarifies the collaboration boundary and prevents diffusion of safety logic across modules.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is measurable, the DP is logically sound and directly traceable, and the IM boundary is well-contained with acceptable diffusion risks. However, the recommended revisions reveal that several design details require clarification and deeper decomposition to mitigate diffusion risks and ensure implementation consistency. Specifically: (1) DP2 (autonomous safety watchdog) requires explicit sub-level design to clarify the boundary between local fallback enforcement and global safety policy; (2) DP3 (channel decomposition) needs architectural specification of the shared buffering framework to prevent code duplication; (3) DP5 (network abstraction layer) requires detailed design of the adapter/facade pattern and its interface contract with Network Link. These are not gate failures but rather signals that the current level is sufficiently validated to proceed with decomposition, and the next level must address the identified diffusion risks and clarify ownership boundaries. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-level FRs is justified to ensure that safety-critical mechanisms (watchdog, constraint enforcement, fallback logic) are properly architected and owned.
next_level_sub_frs: FR2.1: Enforce autonomous safety watchdog that monitors teleoperation link health and latency thresholds, triggering safe fallback to Vehicle when constraints are violated, independent of Safety Services intervention.

FR2.2: Decompose teleoperation command, telemetry, and heartbeat channels into independent functional pathways with dedicated buffering and latency optimization, enabling per-channel tuning without cross-channel interference.

FR2.3: Provide network abstraction layer that decouples remote control logic from transport specifics, enabling adaptive routing and failover selection to minimize end-to-end latency without protocol redesign.

FR2.4: Define and enforce SafetyAlert interface contract that clarifies inputs from Safety Services (constraint limits, watchdog parameters) and outputs to Vehicle (fallback commands, constraint violation alerts), preventing diffusion of safety logic across module boundaries.

revise.why: The integrated DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) is logically sound but requires explicit sub-level design specifications to address diffusion risks and clarify ownership boundaries. Specifically: (1) DP2 must be refined to distinguish between local watchdog enforcement (Teleoperation responsibility) and global safety policy (Safety Services responsibility), with clear SafetyAlert interface semantics; (2) DP3 must specify a shared buffering and latency optimization framework to prevent code duplication across channels; (3) DP5 must document the adapter/facade pattern and its interface contract with Network Link; (4) DP must include explicit scope boundaries to prevent operator UI/UX and vehicle actuation logic from diffusing into Teleoperation. These revisions are not corrections but rather elaborations necessary to guide implementation and mitigate identified risks.

assumptions: Teleoperation module owns all latency-critical buffering, channel decomposition, and network abstraction mechanisms; Safety Services owns global safety policy and constraint definitions; Vehicle owns fallback command execution.

SafetyAlert interface provides the collaboration boundary between Teleoperation (local watchdog enforcement) and Safety Services (global policy provider), with clear directionality and responsibility ownership.
Network Link external interface provides transport abstraction; DP5 (network abstraction layer) is an internal design pattern within Teleoperation that adapts to Network Link, not a separate module.

Operator UI/UX, vehicle actuation, and global safety policy are explicitly out of scope for Teleoperation module; scope is limited to remote control protocol, channel management, latency optimization, and autonomous safety watchdog.

p95 latency ≤ 150 ms is achievable through DP3 (channel decomposition for independent tuning) and DP5 (adaptive routing for path selection), with DP2 (autonomous watchdog) ensuring safe fallback if target is violated.
open_risks: Risk: DP2 autonomous safety watchdog may be perceived as duplicating Safety Services responsibility, creating confusion about ownership and leading to inconsistent constraint enforcement. Mitigation: Sub-level design must explicitly clarify that Teleoperation watchdog is a *local* fallback mechanism triggered by teleoperation link failure; Safety Services owns global policy. SafetyAlert interface contract must define inputs (constraint limits from Safety Services) and outputs (fallback triggers to Vehicle).

Risk: DP3 channel decomposition may result in scattered buffering and latency optimization logic across command uplink, telemetry downlink, and heartbeat implementations, violating DRY principle and creating maintenance burden. Mitigation: Sub-level design must specify a shared buffering and latency optimization framework (e.g., common channel abstraction, utility library) within Teleoperation to ensure consistency and reduce diffusion.

Risk: DP5 network abstraction layer may be confused with a separate networking module or conflated with Network Link external interface, creating ambiguity about internal vs. external boundaries. Mitigation: Sub-level design must document DP5 as an internal adapter/facade pattern that decouples control logic from transport specifics, with explicit interface contract to Network Link.

Risk: Teleoperation module scope may expand to include operator UI/UX, vehicle actuation, or global safety policy, diluting focus on remote control protocol and channel management. Mitigation: Sub-level design must establish and document clear scope boundaries to prevent scope creep.

Risk: Traceability from acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) to specific DP mechanisms may be lost during implementation if sub-level design does not explicitly map each DP contribution to latency targets. Mitigation: Sub-level design must include explicit traceability documentation linking DP3, DP5, and DP2 to latency requirements and verification strategy.
evidence_needed: Sub-level FR and DP specifications for autonomous safety watchdog (FR2.1/DP2.x) that clarify local fallback enforcement vs. global safety policy, with explicit SafetyAlert interface contract (inputs from Safety Services, outputs to Vehicle).

Sub-level FR and DP specifications for channel decomposition (FR2.2/DP3.x) that define shared buffering and latency optimization framework, including common channel abstraction and per-channel tuning mechanisms.
Sub-level FR and DP specifications for network abstraction layer (FR2.3/DP5.x) that document adapter/facade pattern, interface contract with Network Link, and adaptive routing/failover logic.
Sub-level FR and DP specifications for SafetyAlert interface (FR2.4/DP2.x) that define input/output semantics, constraint enforcement logic, and fallback command generation.

Traceability matrix linking p95 latency ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion to DP3 (channel decomposition), DP5 (adaptive routing), and DP2 (watchdog fallback), with verification strategy for each mechanism.

Scope boundary documentation for Teleoperation module (owns: remote control protocol, channel management, latency optimization, network abstraction, autonomous watchdog; does not own: operator UI/UX, vehicle actuation, global safety policy).

Design review evidence confirming that SafetyAlert interface contract prevents diffusion of safety logic across Teleoperation and Safety Services boundaries, with clear ownership of constraint enforcement and policy definition.
